coredata.dc:title: Designing an ultra-sensitive aptasensor based on an AgNPs/thiol-GQD nanocomposite for TNT detection at femtomolar levels using the electrochemical oxidation of Rutin as a redox probe
coredata.dc:creator: Shahdost-fard, Faezeh
Roushani, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, for the first time a highly sensitive and low-cost electrochemical aptasensor was fabricated based on a silver nanoparticles/thiol functionalized graphene quantum dot (AgNPs/thiol-GQD) nanocomposite for the measurement of 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluen (TNT) as a nitroaromatic explosive. For the first time Rutin (RU) as a biological molecule with inherent properties was used as the redox probe in the development of the TNT aptasensor was used. The system was based on a TNT-binding aptamer which is covalently attached onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with the nanocomposite for the formation of a sensing layer and improving the performance of the aptasensor. Using the proposed nanocomposite provides a specific platform with increased surface area which is capable of loading more Aptamer (Ap) molecules as a receptor element of TNT on the electrode surface. So, TNT molecules is in an upward position to be measured and the obtained results indicate that the aptasensor exhibits two wide linear ranges and an unprecedented LOD compared with previously reported analytical methods for TNT detection. Applicability of the developed aptasensor to easily detect TNT in real samples was evaluated. It seems that the proposed strategy can be expanded to other nanoparticles and is expected to have promising implications in the design of electrochemical sensors or biosensors for the detection of various targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
TNT
Explosives
Thiol-GQD
Rutin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation procedure of the Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE as a TNT aptasensor
Characterization and the electrochemical behavior of the developed TNT aptasensor
Investigation of experiment condition for the developed aptasesnor
Investigation of the analytical characteristics of TNT aptasensor
Stability, reproducibility and repeatability of the fabricated aptasensor
Application of the TNT aptasensor in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study has been supported by the Ilam University . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra, (B) TEM image, (C) florescence spectra and (D) UV–vis spectra of the synthesized thiol-GQD.

(A) Nyquist curves of the recorded modified electrode for each steps in: GCE (curve a), thiol-GQD/GCE (curve b), AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE (curve c), Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE (curve d) and the modified GCE after incubation with TNT (curve e). Inset is the equivalent circuit. (B) Recorded CVs for the different steps of the modified electrode (scan rate 40mV s−1).

The DPV results for the developed aptasensor incubated with different concentrations of TNT (A): 0, 0.001, 0.1, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25 and 0.30pM in 0.5mM RU and (B) 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 40 and 45µM in 4mM RU. Also, the corresponding calibration curves between the obtained signal and the TNT concentration are shown.

The DPV results of changes of the proposed aptasensor response after incubation with 1fM TNT (f) and other compounds with similar structure such as (a) 2,4-Dinitrotoluene, (b) p-Toluidine, (c) RDX, (d) 1-Bromo-4-nitrobenzene and (e) 4-Nitroaniline with 103-fold higher concentration than of the TNT.
Schematic illustration of the developed electrochemical aptasensor for TNT detection.
A comparison of the response characteristics of different sensors for TNT detection.

row.entry: Gr d -PANI e -MIP film (Shi et al., 2015)

head.ce:title: Designing an ultra-sensitive aptasensor based on an AgNPs/thiol-GQD nanocomposite for TNT detection at femtomolar levels using the electrochemical oxidation of Rutin as a redox probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ilam, Ilam, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Ilam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, for the first time a highly sensitive and low-cost electrochemical aptasensor was fabricated based on a silver nanoparticles/thiol functionalized graphene quantum dot (AgNPs/thiol-GQD) nanocomposite for the measurement of 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluen (TNT) as a nitroaromatic explosive. For the first time Rutin (RU) as a biological molecule with inherent properties was used as the redox probe in the development of the TNT aptasensor was used. The system was based on a TNT-binding aptamer which is covalently attached onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with the nanocomposite for the formation of a sensing layer and improving the performance of the aptasensor. Using the proposed nanocomposite provides a specific platform with increased surface area which is capable of loading more Aptamer (Ap) molecules as a receptor element of TNT on the electrode surface. So, TNT molecules is in an upward position to be measured and the obtained results indicate that the aptasensor exhibits two wide linear ranges and an unprecedented LOD compared with previously reported analytical methods for TNT detection. Applicability of the developed aptasensor to easily detect TNT in real samples was evaluated. It seems that the proposed strategy can be expanded to other nanoparticles and is expected to have promising implications in the design of electrochemical sensors or biosensors for the detection of various targets.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluen (TNT) as a nitroaromatic explosive is an important environmental, security, and health concern for the global community, because it is the most widely used explosive in industry and military/terrorist activities (Rose et al., 2005). Considering the disadvantages of TNT such as causing severe cardiovascular, abnormal liver function, anemia, cataract and carcinogenic (Nie et al., 2011), distinguish and detecting its residues is of paramount importance in the fight against terrorism. Up to now, some methods such as gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS) (Berg et al., 2007), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Smedts et al., 2003) and Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Dasary et al., 2009) have been commonly performed for TNT detection. Despite their accuracy and reliability, these methods have certain practical disadvantages such as complexity, high cost, and time consummation. Thus, although a significant improvement has been made, still a portable and reliable method for the sensitive and selective measurement of TNT still faces challenges. Currently, TNT detection strategies are trended toward development techniques based on aptasensing methods. As an alternative to the conventional aptasensing procedures, the development of electrochemical aptasensors for the measurement of TNT has some advantages such as selective response, low cost, miniaturization, and simple instrumentation. In the development of electrochemical aptasensing strategies, the signal amplification, sensitivity, and stability are key factors in the improvement of aptasensors performance. Normally, the signal amplification in the electrochemical sesnors and aptasensors can be achieved using nanomaterials such as graphene (Gr) (Wei et al., 2010), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Rohrbach et al., 2012), quantum dots (QDs) (Li et al., 2011; Shahdost-fard and Roushani, 2016), and metal nanoparticles (MNPs) (Su et al., 2013; Roushani and Shahdost-fard, 2016). Among different nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a unique class of nanoparticles (NPs), which has attracted increasing attention among the scientific community, is a good candidate for the construction of electronic devices (Zhu et al., 2012). Due to such unique properties as suitable conductivity, large surface to volume ratio and good biocompatibility, GQDs have been demonstrated to be efficient in biosensing applications, (Shen et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2011) especially in electrochemical sensors (Zhao et al., 2011). Furthermore, coupling noble nanostructures with other compounds and biorecognition elements has had a significant role in various fields, particularly in sensing systems (Gooding et al., 2003). Some of these nanostructures such as metal nanoparticles (MNPs), due to their unique abilities, have received considerable attention in designing sensing methods based on electrochemical sensors (Li and Somorjai, 2010; Shahdost-fard et al., 2014). Among different noble MNPs, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are subjected to new engineering technologies with extraordinarily novel resultant morphologies and characteristics. These NPs have several benefits including simplicity of the preparation process, cost-effective manufacturing method, facilitating more efficient electron transfer (eT), and harboring more active sites on their surface (Yu et al., 2011). These advantages make it a suitable choice, and it seems that utilizing AgNPs along with GQDs as a specific platform can be a good choice in designing electrochemical sensors and aptasensors.

A key factor in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors and aptasensors is achieving a sensitive and stable electrochemical response. For this purpose, a redox probe as an electron mediator should be attached to the surface of an electrode or dissolved in the sensing solution in a cell of electrochemical system. Various redox probes such as catechol (Mazloum-Ardakani and Hosseinzadeh, 2016), riboflavin (Roushani and Shahdost-fard, 2015), and ferrocene and its derivatives (Razumiene et al., 2000) have been used in electrochemical systems. Among different probes, Rutin (RU) as a biological molecule has specific characteristics, but less attention has so far been paid to the fabrication of electrochemical sensors so far (Hendrickson et al., 1994). From the viewpoint of electrochemical reactions, the potential range of hexacyanoferrate ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) as a common redox probe is −0.20 to 0.70V, whereas the potential range of RU is positive (0–0.50V), and the common interferences in this positive range are less. Thus, it seems that the use of RU as a redox probe can be a specific candidate in the development of electrochemical sensors and aptasensors. To the best of our knowledge, utilizing RU probe for the detection of TNT via aptasensing system has not been reported before.l

In the current study, a novel and highly selective aptasensor was developed on an electrode surface for the ultrasensitive detection of TNT through an assembly strategy of the thiol functionalized GQD (thiol-GQD) and AgNPs as an efficient nanocomposite. In the proposed sensing strategy, the TNT amino-aptamer (NH2-Ap) was covalently attached on the AgNPs, which was in turn attached on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by the thiol-GQD. In the presence of TNT molecule as a target, the Ap/TNT complex was formed on the electrode surface and the eT characteristic of RU was changed. Based on our experiments, and also due to the wide targets recognition range of Aps as an inherent advantage, the proposed strategy provides a way to fabricate new aptasensors for other analyses.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.

Utilizing the thiol-GQD in the prepared nanocomposite as a specific platform is a key factor in the fabricating of the proposed aptasensor. The preparation of the thiol-GQD was carried out according to the literature, but with some modification (Minati et al., 2012). In a typical procedure, 10.00mL of HNO3/H2SO4 mixture at a ratio of 1:3 was added to 0.10g multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) powder and was sonicated for 6h at 40°C to achieve oxidized MWCNTs (ox-MWCNTs). The obtained black suspension containing ox-MWCNTs was diluted with distillated water and allowed to stand overnight for precipitation. Then, the supernatant suspension of ox-MWCNTs was removed and, after being washed with distillated water, it was filtered with a filter membrane (0.05µm) under vacuum. 1.00mL of 1.00mM cysteamine solution in ethanol was added to the resulted suspension, mixed (24h), and then decanted overnight. This way, by adding cysteamine, the GQD was isolated on the MWCNTs suspension. After mixing, the black MWCNT slowly precipitated and then the obtained NPs named as oxidized GQD (ox-GQD) were collected. In the next step, 10.00mM of DCC solution in DMF was added to the prepared ox-GQD to start the coupling reaction, and then it was mixed for 24h (at room temperature). Finally, the thiol-GQD was achieved after purifying and isolating. Some techniques were used for confirming the synthesized thiol-GQD.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation procedure of the Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE as a TNT aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: A simple drope-casting technique was used to prepare the TNT aptasensor. Typically, the bare GCE was polished with emery paper with 0.03µm alumina powder and then was briefly sonicated in a mixture of deionized water and absolute ethanol to remove the absorbed particles. After the cleaned GCE was dried at room temperature, it was coated by casting 10.00µL of the synthesized thiol-GQD and then dried in the air overnight. Subsequently, 10.00µL of the synthesized AgNPs was dropped onto the surface of t explained in the he resulting thiol-GQD/GCE. After 4h when the AgNPs was attached on the thiol groups of the thiol-GQD/GCE surface, 10.00µL of the NH2-Ap solution was incubated onto the surface of the obtained AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE. Thus the amine functional groups of Ap were immobilized onto the AgNPs onto the AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE surface. Afterwards, the modified electrode, which is denoted as Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE, was rinsed with PBS to eliminate the unbounded substrate. Finally, the achieved TNT aptasesnor was used for the electrochemical experiments. By incubating TNT as a target, the Ap/TNT complex was formed on the prepared aptasensor surface and the changes of the electrochemical signal of RU were monitored. Scheme 1 depicts the mechanism of the proposed strategy in the fabrication of the electrochemical aptasensor for TNT detection.

Obviously, using the thiol-GQDs as an initial layer in the prepared nanocomposite is a key factor in modifying the electrode surface and signal improvement. The synthesis of the thiol-GQDs was conducted via MWCNTs as a primary substance, and under chemical reaction the ox-GQD was achieved. The ox-GQD was converted to the thiol-GQD in the presence of a coupling agent. Purifying and isolating steps in this experiment are very important in this experiment. Several times washing of the MWCNTs precipitated with DMF may increase the amount of the thiol-GQDs isolated from the MWCNTs. It is notable that the presence of the carboxylated carbonaceous fragments can hamper the sidewall functionalization of the MWCNTs (Verdejo et al., 2007). This point is a crucial factor in experiments, especially for biological applications. In this reaction the cysteamine acts as an exfoliation agent causing the detachment of nano-graphene fragments, which is produced by the oxidation reaction. The detachment of the GQD from MWCNTs surface may have two reasons: (1) these nano-graphene fragments are linked to the MWCNTs backbone through π-π staking interaction. This π-π coupling can be reduced by the intercalation of small molecules of cysteamine between the GQD and the MWCNT sidewall and lead to the detachment of the GQD; (2) the high oxidation of the MWCNTs can play an important role in the process of the detachment of the GQD from the MWCNTs surface. The ox-MWCNTs can form stable colloidal suspensions in many solvents such as water, DMF, and ethanol, but the thiol-GQD suspensions are stable in ethanol and DMF. Since the water solubility of the thiol-GQD is lower than that of the ox-GQD, it can be a good platform for designing sensors and aptasensors. We used some techniques to successfully synthesize the thiol-GQD; the results are shown in Fig. 1. According to Fig. 1A, the existence of an absorbance band at 2507cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of the thiol-GQD manifested successful functionalization of the GQD with the thiol groups. The absorbance band at 3508cm−1 is ascribed to hydroxyl groups in the thiol-GQD; also, the bands at 2862 and 2933cm−1 are related to aliphatic C-H bands, respectively. Furthermore, TEM technique was used to confirm the nanostructure synthesis of the thiol-GQD. The TEM image in Fig. 1B corroborated the synthesis of these nanoparticles with nanosize structure, which could serve as a good way for increasing the surface area of the modified electrode. In order to examine closer the prepared thiol-GQD, fluorescent measurement was performed in the range of 400–800nm with the excitation wavelength at 470nm. Fig. 1C shows an intense emission which is in agreement with other results reported in the literature of the fluorescent GQDs (Minati et al., 2012). Additionally, the UV–vis absorption peak in DMF (Fig. 1D) was observed, which is also reported for some carbon dots spectra (Wang et al., 2010). All performed tests in this section can be a confirmation of the successful synthesis of the thiol-GQD.

The synthesized AgNPs was studied by some characterization techniques. The morphology of the spherical AgNPs was investigated with TEM image (Fig. S1A). The diameter of these NPs has a size range from 4nm to around 20nm with the average size of ca. 10nm. Furthermore, the UV–vis technique was used as another evidence for the synthesized AgNPs. Also, the UV–vis spectrum of the prepared AgNPs was recorded in the range of 300–700nm. As can be seen in Fig. S1B, the successful synthesis of these NPs can be confirmed by the absorbance peak which is approximately at 400nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and the electrochemical behavior of the developed TNT aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The TNT aptasensor fabrication process was probed by recording stepwise modification of the modified electrode in the 0.1mM RU. Because the RU is a bulky molecule and has many oxygen atoms, it is considered to have negative partial charge. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a characterization technique that provides electrical information in the frequency domain (Macdonal et al., 1987). We used EIS to generate the Nyquist plots of the faradic impedance spectra at the different electrodes. Fig. 2A (a–e) shows EIS plots obtained at a potential of 0.2V for bare GCE (a), thiol-GQD/GCE (b), AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE (c), Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE (d), and TNT/Ap/AgNPs/thiol-GQD/GCE (e). According to this technique the charge transfer resistance, Rct, is a key parameter for the kinetic of a probe at the electrode interface. This parameter showed the immobilization of the substrates onto the modified electrode surface in each step of the process. As shown in Fig. 2A, by pipetting some of the thiol-GQDs on the GCE surface, Rct value increased from ~400.50Ω for the bare GCE (curve a) to ~33.14kΩ for the thiol-GQD/GCE (curve b). The existence of the thiol and hydroxyl groups in the structure of the thiol-GQD led to the negative partial charge of this nanomaterial. So the presence of the thiol-GQDs on the GCE surface led to the repulsion of the redox probe and decreased of eT. By adding one drop of the prepared AgNPs onto the surface of the thiol-GQD/GCE, Rct value increased to ~50.40kΩ (curve c). In this step the presence of the AgNPs with negative charge, immobilized onto the thiol groups of the GQDs via the bonding formation of Ag-S, led to the repulsion between the AgNPs and the redox probe and thereby the Rct value was increased. By loading the TNT specific Ap onto the electrode surface with chemisorption, as expected according to curve d, the Rct value significantly increased to ~76.60kΩ. This increase in value indicated that the covalent attachment of the amino-Ap onto the AgNPs surface took place via the formation of a favored Ag-NH2 bond between the NH2 group of the Ap and the AgNPs. Due to the negative charges of the phosphate groups in the Ap molecules, the eT of the redox probe was prevented and the extent of the probe response was reduced. By incubating TNT as a target onto the surface of the modified electrode, the semicircle diameter of the plot increased to ~88.81kΩ (curve e). The obtained Rct value in this step indicated the formation of the TNT/Ap complex on the modified electrode surface, which induced folding and conformational change in the Ap structure. Thus, the eT of the redox probe was prevented from the electrode surface. Therefore, the increased resistance in each step of the aptasensor fabrication can be related to impeding eT at the electrode surface in curve b and e and the electrostatic repulsion between the negative charge of the electrode surface and the probe in curve c and d. The obtained EIS results confirmed the successful fabrication of the proposed aptasensor. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was used to further confirm the layer by layer immobilization of the modified electrode, was used. Fig. 2B shows the typical CV responses of the modified electrodes in each step in the RU solution (0.10mM). First, a cyclic voltammogram related to the reversible redox response of RU was observed at the bare electrode (GCE) (voltammogram a). Secondly, after dropping the thiol-GQD on the surface of GCE, the peak current decreased which is attributed to the repel between the negative partial charge of the thiol-GQD and the bulky molecule of the probe. Thus, eT between the redox probe and the electrode surface was inhibited (voltammogram b). Thirdly, the immobilization of the AgNPs onto the thiol groups of the thio-GQD, which forms a self-assemble monolayer via Ag-S bonds onto the surface of the thiol-GQD/GCE, prevented eT of RU. Thus, an increase in the peak potential separation accrued to the decrease of the peak current (voltammogram c). Finally, the presence of the Ap molecules comprising 100 nucleotides, which were covalently grafted on the modified GCE surface, formed a cluster of long spacer arms on the platform, resulting in the more blocking of the electron flow of RU to the electrode surface (voltammogram d). The herein obtained CV data corroborate the preceding results obtained by EIS and both of them confirm that the sensing interface is successfully achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of experiment condition for the developed aptasesnor
ce:section.ce:para: This section is presented in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the analytical characteristics of TNT aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the ability of the designed aptasensor to detect TNT, a series of different concentrations of TNT was measured under the optimal conditions and the analytical calibrations were obtained to evaluate their sensitivity. Fig. 3 shows the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) responses of the RU probe related to the addition of the different concentrations of TNT. It was notable that the increase in the concentration of TNT decreased the peak currents, which was due to the formation of an increasing number of TNT/Ap complexes, resulting in the enhanced hindering of the eT reaction of RU on the sensing surface. The dependence of the DPV current response on the TNT concentration is also presented (Fig. 3). Each point on the calibration curves corresponds to the mean values obtained from three independent measurements. According to the calibrations, good linearity was obtained between the current signal of RU and the concentration of TNT: for lower concentrations from 0.001pM to 0.300pM with the regression equation ΔI (µA)=0.7221 [TNT] (pM)+0.1208 (R2=0.9924) and for higher concentrations from 5µM to 45µM with the regression equation ΔI (µA)=0.080 [TNT] (µM)–0.078 (R2=0.996). All of measurements were repeated for five times and presented with relative standard deviations (RSD%) of 2%.

The estimated limit of detection (LOD) at the signal to noise ratio of 3 was 0.33fM. This value of LOD the surpasses achieved detection limits for TNT detection by various methods, as well as most of the previously reported TNT sensors and aptasensors in the literature as clearly shown in Table 1. The results presented in this table obviously show that the designed aptasensor, which was based on the thiol-GQD/AgNPs nanocomposite as a unique platform, displayed better response characteristics compared with the other sensors and aptasensors. The high sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed strategy relies upon some amplification features such as: (1) using the thiol-GQDs with some inherent excellent properties, including high surface to volume ratio which can provide a high density of the AgNPs on the modified GCE surface; (2) adding AgNPs on the surface of the thiol-GQDs leads to the formation of a self-assemble monolayer onto the surface of the thiol-GQD modified GCE; so, it is capable of loading more Ap molecules as a receptor element of TNT on the modified electrode surface (3) The covalent attachment of the amino-Ap onto the AgNPs surface takes place via the formation of a favored Ag-NH2 bond of the NH2 groups of Ap and the AgNPs onto the electrode surface; (4) utilizing RU as an active probe in the positive potential range leads to the decrease of the interferences in proposed aptasensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and repeatability of the fabricated aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: This section is presented in Supplementary material.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the designed method towards TNT detection in the presence of compounds with similar structures is an important parameter. The structure of DNT closely resembles that of TNT and this compound is found to be unavailable impurity in the military grade TNT (Pamula and Fair, 2000). RDX as an explosive nitroamine which is more powerful than TNT, is widely used in military and industrial applications (Czerski and Proud, 2007). 4-Nitroaniline, 1-Bromo-4-nitrobenzene, and p-Toluidine have a similar structure towards TNT. Therefore, it is very valuable to confirm the selectivity of the developed TNT aptasensor over the possible interference in order to avoid false identification. For this purpose, under optimized experiment conditions, 10.00µL of each compound such as RDX, 4-Nitroaniline, 1-Bromo-4-nitrobenzene, p-Toluidine and DNT was separately incubated onto the surface of the prepared aptasensor. Then the typical DPV responses of TNT and the different interferences were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4, by incubating 1.00fM TNT, a well-defined response was observed at the proposed aptasensor, whereas in the presence of 1.00pM of different compounds no significant response was observed. These results are in agreement with the fact that the binding event between the Ap and TNT is based on a selective recognition between them, not on nonspecific adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the TNT aptasensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: This section is presented in Supplementary material.

In summary, a novel aptasensor was fabricated for the detection of trace amounts of TNT based on the covalent attachment of Ap onto the AgNPs/thiol-GQD nanocomposite as a unique platform, and RU was applied as an electrochemical indicator. Upon the binding of TNT to the surface of the Ap-modified electrode, the complex of TNT/Ap was formed onto the modified electrode surface, which induced folding and conformational change in the Ap structure. So the eT of RU was inhibited, and the DPV signal related to RU was decreased. The interaction between the Ap and TNT via this developed method was investigated by CV, EIS, and DPV techniques. The detection sensitivity of the designed aptasensor is favorably comparable to other reported TNT sensors and aptasensors. The feasibility of using this aptasensor was demonstrated by determining TNT in soil and water samples with obtained satisfactory results. The reasons behind these good results were mainly attributed to the superior properties of the prepared nanocomposite, the large amount of the immobilized Ap on its surface, and finally selection of a specific redox probe in the proposed aptasensing strategy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study has been supported by the Ilam University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.09.048.
